text: The weird thing about AI progress is that the flashy demo is usually the least important part. The boring loop is where the leverage sits.
Most teams say they want speed. What they actually want is motion without the emotional cost of committing to a direction.
If a process only works when a senior person is watching, you don't have a process yet. You have theater.
Small evals are underrated. Not because they are perfect, but because they force you to cash out your taste into something testable.
A lot of 'strategy' is just choosing which problems you are willing to look stupid on for six months.
The longer I work on systems, the less I trust explanations that sound smooth on first read. Good systems usually feel slightly awkward before they feel obvious.
The useful question isn't 'can the model do it?' It's 'can the loop keep doing it after the novelty wears off?'
You can get surprisingly far by shrinking the problem until feedback gets cheap.

Quick note on the retrieval work: the latest pass is finally stable enough that I trust the trend, but not yet stable enough that I'd call the numbers final. I want one more cleanup pass on the labels before we present anything broadly.

Sharing a rough instinct here: I think we're still mixing two different goals in the planning doc. One is shipping something usable next month, the other is proving the long-term architecture. Both are valid, but the current draft keeps pretending they're the same thing.
I don't think this needs a bigger rewrite yet. I'd rather make the evaluation loop less annoying first and then see which parts still feel painful after a week of actual use.
For tomorrow, my preference is a short working session over a presentation. We already know the broad shape of the problem. What we need now is faster contact with the edge cases.
Tentative call: let's keep the scope narrow for v1. The broader concept is interesting, but I don't want us paying the complexity cost before we know where the value is.
Status update: the core path is working, but the failure modes are still too opaque. I'm less worried about raw capability than I am about debugging cost.
